issue_title: Empty cards dialog has white background in Black theme

reasoning_trace.finding: The symptom is theme-specific UI rendering: in Black theme, selecting Deck picker -> Check -> Empty cards shows a dialog whose content/panel background is white instead of dark. The likely API surface is the DeckPicker menu action and the Empty Cards dialog creation/theme path.

Initial recursive search with rg failed because rg is unavailable; PowerShell search found Empty Cards resources and concrete source files, including EmptyCardsDialogFragment.kt, EmptyCardsViewModel.kt, and DeckPicker.kt.
The referenced screenshot directory was not present under the current repo root, so the analysis proceeded from the source tree and the report's visual description.
DeckPicker.kt imports EmptyCardsDialogFragment, assigns the menu title for R.id.action_empty_cards, and handles that menu item by showing a new EmptyCardsDialogFragment.

In onOptionsItemSelected, the R.id.action_empty_cards branch logs the click and calls EmptyCardsDialogFragment().show(supportFragmentManager, EmptyCardsDialogFragment.TAG), establishing DeckPicker as the entry Activity and the fragment as the dialog entry point.

EmptyCardsViewModel only performs the collection query via withCol { getEmptyCards() } and emits SearchingForEmptyCards, EmptyCardsSearchResult, or EmptyCardsSearchFailure. It does not set UI colors or theme attributes.

EmptyCardsDialogFragment inflates DialogEmptyCardsBinding, binds state, starts the empty-card search, then creates the dialog with androidx.appcompat.app.AlertDialog.Builder(requireContext()).show { setTitle(...); setView(binding.root) ... }.
The custom dialog layout root is a FrameLayout with no android:background. Its child containers also do not force a white background, so a white panel is unlikely to originate from this layout XML.
The app's Material dialog background drawable uses ?attr/colorSurfaceContainerHigh, which should resolve to a dark color in Black theme.

ThemeOverlay.AnkiDroid.AlertDialog extends ThemeOverlay.Material3.MaterialAlertDialog and sets android:windowBackground to @drawable/material_dialog_background. This is the intended themed dialog background path.

Base.Theme.Dark.Black sets colorSurfaceContainerHigh to #151515, android:colorBackground and windowBackground to black, and alertDialogTheme to ThemeOverlay.AnkiDroid.AlertDialog.Black. ThemeOverlay.AnkiDroid.AlertDialog.Black sets android:colorBackground to theme_black_primary_light.
The non-black dark theme also uses alertDialogTheme @style/ThemeOverlay.AnkiDroid.AlertDialog, confirming the app expects AlertDialogs to use the custom Material3 overlay.

The local AlertDialog.Builder.show extension only applies the block and calls Builder.show(); it does not correct or apply any background. Therefore the chosen Builder implementation controls whether the Material dialog background is used.

Several modern dialogs in the same package use MaterialAlertDialogBuilder(requireContext()), while EmptyCardsDialogFragment uses androidx.appcompat.app.AlertDialog.Builder. This is a notable mismatch for a Material3-themed app dialog.

BrowserOptionsDialog is a comparable custom-view dialog: it inflates a binding and returns MaterialAlertDialogBuilder(requireContext()).create { setView(binding.root) ... }, showing the local pattern for custom Material dialogs.
ForgetCardsDialog also returns MaterialAlertDialogBuilder(requireContext()).create { ... }, reinforcing that MaterialAlertDialogBuilder is the expected builder for themed app dialogs.
FieldSelectionDialog uses MaterialAlertDialogBuilder(requireContext()).create for a simple list dialog, confirming this is not limited to custom layouts.

The layout does not hard-code @android:color/white, @color/white, or ?android:attr/colorBackground. Since the white background is visible behind the dialog content, the fault is more likely the dialog container/window background than the Empty Cards layout.

Black theme values are dark: colorSurfaceContainerHigh is #151515 and android:colorBackground is black. The theme resources themselves do not explain a white dialog if the correct Material overlay is used.

reasoning_trace.target: EmptyCardsDialogFragment uses AppCompat AlertDialog.Builder instead of MaterialAlertDialogBuilder
reasoning_trace.finding: The old AppCompat builder is the only Empty Cards-specific rendering decision that bypasses the MaterialAlertDialogBuilder path used by neighboring dialogs. It can leave the dialog panel/content background on the default light AppCompat styling instead of the app's dark Material surface under Black theme.

The user action directly constructs EmptyCardsDialogFragment; onCreateDialog immediately creates the AlertDialog via AlertDialog.Builder(requireContext()), attaches the custom view, and displays it. That builder selection is in the causal path before any state-specific content is shown.

fault_candidates.reasoning: The Empty Cards dialog is created with androidx.appcompat.app.AlertDialog.Builder while comparable Material3 dialogs in the same app use MaterialAlertDialogBuilder. The Empty Cards layout does not set a white background, and Black theme resources define dark dialog surface colors, so the most likely cause is this builder choosing the wrong dialog implementation/background path and producing a light default panel in Black theme.

final_answer.causal_chain: The DeckPicker Check -> Empty cards menu item matches R.id.action_empty_cards and calls EmptyCardsDialogFragment().show(...). EmptyCardsDialogFragment.onCreateDialog inflates dialog_empty_cards.xml, whose root has no explicit white background. The method then creates the dialog with AppCompat AlertDialog.Builder(requireContext()) and sets the custom view. Because this bypasses the MaterialAlertDialogBuilder path used elsewhere, the dialog container/window can keep a light default AppCompat background instead of resolving the Black theme Material surface background, so the user sees a white dialog background around the Empty Cards content.
final_answer.fix_hint: Create the Empty Cards dialog with MaterialAlertDialogBuilder(requireContext()) instead of AppCompat AlertDialog.Builder so it uses the app's Material3 alert-dialog background in Black theme.
